skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Automates browser sessions through inference.sh using Playwright-style actions. It can open pages, inspect element refs, interact with forms, run page JavaScript, capture media, upload files, and use proxies.
skill.summary: Automate browser navigation, form workflows, screenshots, and web data capture through inference.sh.
skill.author: inf-sh

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer correctly identified extensive external command, network, filesystem, proxy, and credential-adjacent behavior, but most detections are documented examples and shell templates for the intended browser automation workflow. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, but the skill exposes high-risk capabilities including authenticated browsing, cookie extraction examples, arbitrary page JavaScript, proxy rotation, screenshots/video, and local file upload.

high_findings.title: Authenticated Session Data Exposure Risk
high_findings.description: The skill documents automated login flows, credential entry, session reuse, and cookie extraction. This is an intended browser automation feature, but it can expose authenticated page data, cookies, or credentials if used on sensitive accounts or untrusted sites.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced documentation explicitly fills password fields, keeps authenticated sessions, and prints cookies. The behavior is documented rather than hidden, so the risk is capability misuse rather than confirmed malicious intent.

high_findings.title: Arbitrary Page JavaScript Execution
high_findings.description: The execute function accepts JavaScript code and runs it in the browser page context. This can extract page content, trigger hidden actions, or read data available to page scripts when used against authenticated or sensitive pages.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command reference states that user-supplied JavaScript is executed on the page, and templates use it for extraction. This is a legitimate feature but materially increases security impact.

high_findings.title: Proxy Rotation and Anonymity Support
high_findings.description: The proxy documentation includes authenticated proxies, SOCKS5 proxies, privacy-focused routing, and rotating proxies for scraping. These features can be legitimate for testing, but they can also enable abuse, evasion, or policy violations.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly documents proxy credentials, rate-limit avoidance, and SOCKS5 routing. The examples are clear and intentional, but no malicious destination or exfiltration endpoint is present.

medium_findings.title: Shell Templates Run External Browser Commands
medium_findings.description: The included templates are executable shell scripts that call the infsh CLI with user-provided URLs and session IDs. This is expected for the skill, but it creates operational risk if users run templates with untrusted input or broad credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts directly invoke an external CLI and accept URL arguments. The commands are visible workflow templates, and I did not find evidence of hidden command execution.

medium_findings.title: Screenshots and Videos May Capture Sensitive Data
medium_findings.description: The skill can capture screenshots and session videos, including authenticated workflows. The documentation warns against recording sensitive sessions, but the feature can still preserve credentials, account data, or private page content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Video and screenshot capture are documented capabilities, and the reference file explicitly notes sensitive-session concerns. This is a real privacy risk with legitimate uses.

medium_findings.title: Local File Upload Capability
medium_findings.description: The interact action supports uploading files from local file paths to web forms. This is useful for automation, but a mistaken or malicious prompt could cause sensitive local files to be submitted to a website.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The upload action and file_paths parameter are explicitly documented. The risk depends on user approval and target site behavior, so confidence is high for capability risk but not malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Static Markdown Command Detections Are Mostly Intended Examples
low_findings.description: Many external command findings come from fenced Markdown examples showing how to call infsh, jq, curl, or helper tools. These examples are visible documentation for the skill workflow rather than concealed execution paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious syntax appears inside documentation examples and matches the stated purpose of a CLI-driven browser skill. The executable templates are covered separately as medium risk.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal and Weak Crypto Static Hits Appear to Be False Positives
low_findings.description: The path traversal indicators correspond to relative documentation links, and weak crypto indicators appear to be scanner matches on documentation text rather than cryptographic implementation. No evidence found of actual traversal logic or cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation links and reference tables, not code that opens paths or computes hashes. Confidence is limited because the static report aggregates many weak-crypto matches without snippets.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Demonstration Targets
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs in the documentation use example domains, public test endpoints, or placeholder sites to demonstrate browser automation. No evidence found of a hidden exfiltration endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are embedded in usage examples and align with the browser automation domain. I did not find suspicious webhook, paste, or command-and-control destinations.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on repeated infsh CLI calls from Markdown examples and shell templates. Users should review commands before running templates and avoid passing untrusted values.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and central to the skill design. It is not hidden, but it executes external commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser JavaScript Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The execute function runs user-provided JavaScript in the target page. This should be limited to trusted pages and reviewed extraction logic.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The function contract directly supports arbitrary JavaScript strings. The risk is inherent to the capability.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential and Cookie Handling
dangerous_patterns.description: Authentication examples handle passwords, TOTP secrets, cookies, and session identifiers. These values should be treated as secrets and not logged or recorded.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation names and uses credential-bearing values directly. The file also includes best practices, but exposure risk remains.

dangerous_patterns.title: Proxy Routing
dangerous_patterns.description: Proxy and SOCKS settings can mask origin traffic or route requests through third-party networks. Publish only with clear acceptable-use warnings.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Proxy support is explicitly documented, including SOCKS5. Legitimate testing use is possible, so this is a controlled-use risk.

content.value_statement: Manual web workflows are slow to repeat and hard to verify. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured browser workflow for navigation, form actions, screenshots, videos, and extraction.
actual_capabilities: Opens browser sessions for a target URL through the infsh agent-browser app.
Returns compact element refs such as @e1 for clicking, filling, selecting, and dragging.
Captures page snapshots, screenshots, full-page screenshots, and session videos.
Runs JavaScript in the page context for targeted data extraction or page inspection.
Supports authenticated workflows, proxy settings, file uploads, and session reuse patterns.
Provides shell templates for form automation, content capture, and authenticated sessions.
limitations: It depends on the inference.sh CLI and an available agent-browser backend.
Element refs can become stale after navigation or dynamic page changes.
The documentation notes iframe limitations and suggests JavaScript workarounds.
Sensitive pages require careful handling because screenshots, videos, cookies, and uploads can expose private data.

use_cases.title: Automate QA Browser Checks
use_cases.description: Run repeatable login, form, navigation, screenshot, and video workflows for web application verification.

use_cases.title: Capture Web Research Evidence
use_cases.description: Open pages, extract visible text and links, and save screenshots for later review.

use_cases.title: Prototype Agent Browsing Flows
use_cases.description: Give an agent compact element refs and browser actions without writing a custom Playwright integration.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Inspect a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open this URL with agent-browser, summarize the page title, visible text, and available interactive element refs: [URL].

prompt_templates.title: Fill a Test Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser to inspect this form, identify the required refs, fill test data, submit it, and verify the result: [URL].

prompt_templates.title: Capture Page Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser to capture a snapshot, full-page screenshot, visible text, and all links from this page: [URL]. Save outputs with clear names.

prompt_templates.title: Run an Authenticated Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser for this authenticated workflow. Ask before entering secrets, avoid video recording, verify login success, complete these steps, then close the session: [steps].

output_examples.input: Inspect a pricing page and identify the buttons a user can click.
output_examples.output: A concise page summary with the title, main pricing sections, and element refs for plan selection, contact sales, and navigation links.

output_examples.input: Submit a demo contact form with test data and confirm success.
output_examples.output: A workflow report describing the fields filled, the submit action taken, the final page state, and whether a success message appeared.

output_examples.input: Capture evidence from a product documentation page.
output_examples.output: A saved screenshot, extracted page text summary, link inventory, and notes about any dynamic content that required a fresh snapshot.

best_practices: Use fresh snapshots after navigation, form submission, modal changes, or dynamic loading.
Keep credentials in environment variables and avoid recording sessions that contain sensitive data.
Close sessions after each workflow unless you intentionally need authenticated session reuse.
anti_patterns: Do not run templates against untrusted URLs without reviewing the commands and outputs.
Do not upload local files unless you have verified the target site and file path.
Do not use proxy rotation to bypass site rules, access controls, or rate limits.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It automates browser navigation, element interactions, screenshots, videos, page snapshots, JavaScript execution, and file uploads through inference.sh.

faq.question: Does it require inference.sh?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples and templates use the infsh CLI and the agent-browser application.

faq.question: Can it work with authenticated sites?
faq.answer: Yes, but use extra care. Avoid recording sensitive sessions and protect passwords, cookies, and session IDs.

faq.question: Why do element refs change?
faq.answer: Refs are tied to a page snapshot. Navigation, form submissions, and dynamic content can invalidate old refs.

faq.question: Can it scrape web pages?
faq.answer: Yes. It can extract visible text, links, and selected data, subject to site terms and access rules.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish without warnings?
faq.answer: No. The skill has legitimate uses, but it needs strong warnings for credentials, cookies, recording, file uploads, JavaScript execution, and proxies.
